askNext: What exact meaning of R2 is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
r2.body: R2 earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
r2.example: "R2 came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
r2.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
r2.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

r2.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
r2.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Cloudflare often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: Is Rabbit interesting because of the hardware, the action layer, or the marketing?
Does it do something meaningfully better than a phone plus apps?
Is the value in the device, or in the orchestration layer behind it?
rabbit.body:
Rabbit is useful partly because it makes an old ambition newly legible. People do not merely want smarter chat. They want fewer menus, fewer tabs, and fewer tiny acts of digital housekeeping.

The risk, as ever, is that "fewer apps" becomes "one more device".
rabbit.example: "The demo made Rabbit look like the future. The harder question was whether that future needed another piece of hardware in your pocket."
rabbit.misuse: By talking about Rabbit as if it solved agentic computing in general, rather than shipping one very visible attempt at turning AI into a standalone consumer device. The company is a case study, not a general law of the interface future.
rabbit.note: Rabbit's own positioning has shifted over time. The original story centred on rabbitOS and a Large Action Model; the current product pages also emphasise rabbitOS 2, DLAM, and third-party agent integrations on r1.
rabbit.practicalMeaning: A bet that people want a dedicated AI-native device, not just another app on a phone. Also a bet that action matters more than chat, and that orchestration can be packaged as consumer hardware without becoming a niche curiosity.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A consumer device trying to make AI action feel native enough to justify existing separately from the phone.

rabbit.warningLabel: Often discussed as if it were a general theory of AI devices, rather than one company shipping one very opinionated product.
rabbit.whyExists: Because every few years the industry rediscovers the dream of a dedicated personal assistant device and insists that this time it will be different.

askNext: How are documents chunked, embedded, retrieved, and filtered?
What evidence shows the retrieved material is actually relevant?
What happens when retrieval fails or returns contradictory context?
rag.body: RAG is valuable because it is ordinary. It lets you improve answers with better retrieval, better curation, and better context handling rather than pretending the base model magically absorbed your SharePoint.
rag.example: "The team said they had solved hallucinations with RAG. What they had actually solved was the first paragraph of the sales demo."
rag.misuse: People describe almost any search-plus-model feature as RAG, even when retrieval quality is poor, documents are shoved into the prompt indiscriminately, or there is no meaningful grounding at all. Slapping documents near a model is not the same as building a reliable retrieval system.
rag.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means chunking documents, creating embeddings, storing vectors, retrieving candidates, reranking or filtering them, and then deciding what enters the prompt. The model is only as grounded as those earlier steps allow.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The practical way to make a general model look domain-aware.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A search and context assembly problem sitting in front of a model call.

rag.whyExists: Base models do not reliably know your internal documents, latest policies, or private data. RAG exists so a system can supplement the model with fresh, domain-specific, or proprietary information at query time.

askNext: Which AI surfaces are being measured?
Are they tracking citations, mentions, referrals, conversions, or ordinary rankings?
What evidence shows the work changes AI answers rather than just producing more SEO copy?
ranked-ai.body: The phrase "rank in AI" is useful mostly because it reveals who has mistaken a probability cloud for a leaderboard.
ranked-ai.example: "Ranked AI promised visibility in AI search, which sounded new until the work plan included clearer pages, better citations, and the old religion of making useful content."
ranked-ai.misuse: It is easy to sell as a guaranteed way to "rank in AI", as if assistants had one stable leaderboard. In reality, AI visibility depends on source quality, structured information, authority, citations, query phrasing, and the messy retrieval habits of each system.
ranked-ai.practicalMeaning: In practice Ranked AI belongs to the wider GEO and AI SEO market: audits, content optimisation, citation strategy, monitoring, and the hard work of making a brand easier for answer engines to understand and trust.

translations.label: agency meaning
translations.text: Make the brand easier for answer engines to find, cite, and recommend.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A newer wrapper around discoverability work, with some real AI-search monitoring and plenty of old SEO discipline underneath.

ranked-ai.warningLabel: AI search visibility is real. Guaranteed AI ranking is usually theatre.
ranked-ai.whyExists: It exists because brands are learning that being visible in Google results is no longer the whole discovery problem. AI answers, summaries, citations, and recommendations increasingly shape whether a business is mentioned at all.

askNext: Which limit are we hitting: requests, input tokens, output tokens, concurrency, spend, or context?
Do we have backoff, queueing, fallbacks, or a cheaper model tier for overflow?
Is the limit a provider constraint, a workspace setting, or self-inflicted traffic design?
rate-limit.body: Rate limits are rarely glamorous. They are, however, one of the first signs that a prototype has wandered into production clothing.
rate-limit.example: "The demo scaled beautifully until the rate limit arrived and taught the agent the ancient discipline of waiting."
rate-limit.misuse: It gets confused with spend limits, context limits, or general product restrictions. Those may interact, but they are not the same thing: one controls throughput, another controls money, and another controls how much the model can read at once.
rate-limit.note: Usage limits is the umbrella phrase people often use in conversation. For engineering decisions, separate rate limits, spend limits, context limits, and feature caps before designing the workaround.
rate-limit.practicalMeaning: In practice it means backoff logic, queues, retries, request shaping, model tier choices, budget alerts, and the discovery that your "real-time" agent is now waiting politely behind its own success.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The service is not down; it is declining to be consumed quite so rapidly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A throughput ceiling that becomes an architecture concern as soon as the product works.

rate-limit.whyExists: It exists to protect shared infrastructure, control abuse, preserve service quality, and make sure one ambitious integration does not turn a provider's capacity plan into a public experiment.

askNext: What kinds of tasks genuinely improve when the model spends longer reasoning?
How much latency and cost does a higher setting add in production?
Are you buying better performance, or just a slower answer with nicer posture?
reasoning-effort.body: The term is genuinely practical. It only becomes ridiculous when people start treating a compute budget as a personality trait.
reasoning-effort.example: "They turned reasoning effort to high for a simple extraction job, which was a costly way to discover the value of restraint."
reasoning-effort.misuse: It is often treated as a truth slider. Higher effort can improve performance on difficult tasks, but it does not guarantee correctness, judgement, or freedom from the model's usual bad habits.
reasoning-effort.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means deciding whether the task is worth the latency and token burn. Low effort is sensible for routine work. Higher effort is better reserved for coding, planning, maths, and the kinds of questions people later pretend were obvious.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A user-facing way to expose the trade-off between speed, cost, and extra internal computation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A knob for deciding when a task deserves more machine time than your budget wanted to give it.

reasoning-effort.whyExists: It exists because not every prompt deserves the same amount of computational attention. A quick classification task and a multi-step coding problem should not be billed as though they require identical intellectual theatre.

askNext: What kinds of tasks measurably improve when the system takes longer?
How much extra latency and cost are we accepting for that gain?
Is the task truly hard, or merely badly specified?
reasoning-model.body: The machine may take longer. That does not mean it has become profound.
reasoning-model.example: "They upgraded to the reasoning model and were delighted by the improved maths, right up to the point they asked it a vague strategic question."
reasoning-model.misuse: By treating "reasoning" as a synonym for correctness, judgement, or wisdom. Extra deliberation can improve results, but it can also produce a longer route to the same wrong conclusion.
reasoning-model.note: The phrase is useful if it names a measurable operating trade-off. It becomes perfume when it is used as a personality trait.
reasoning-model.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slower responses, higher cost, and better odds on thorny tasks if the problem is well framed and the evaluation is honest.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A premium mode for harder questions and a premium invoice for asking them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A slower model path that can improve structured task performance without guaranteeing sound judgement.

reasoning-model.whyExists: Because not every prompt is a pattern-matching snack. Some tasks benefit when the system takes more time, explores alternatives, or uses tools before committing to an answer.

askNext: How does the provider define and expose reasoning-token usage?
What quality gain justified the extra latency and spend?
Can the task be scoped more tightly so the model thinks less and performs better?
reasoning-tokens.body: The useful question is not whether the model thought. The useful question is whether the additional hidden work improved anything a customer could notice.
reasoning-tokens.example: "The answer looked short, but the usage report showed enough reasoning tokens to suggest the model had held an expensive internal committee meeting first."
reasoning-tokens.misuse: By talking as though more hidden tokens automatically mean better judgement. Sometimes the model is genuinely doing harder work. Sometimes it is taking the scenic route to the wrong answer and charging for the view.
reasoning-tokens.note: Providers do not expose this concept consistently. Sometimes it is a direct meter, sometimes a vague usage hint, and sometimes mostly a marketing aroma around slower models.
reasoning-tokens.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slower responses, higher usage cost, budget controls for internal deliberation, and partial visibility at best into where the extra compute went.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The model appears to have thought harder than the budget approved.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Extra compute was burned somewhere between the prompt and the answer, and you may only get partial receipts.

reasoning-tokens.whyExists: Because reasoning-oriented model modes often consume more compute than the final response length suggests, and providers need some way to describe or meter the extra budget.

askNext: Are users seeing the raw intermediate steps, a provider summary, or nothing at all?
Does the extra reasoning budget improve outcomes on this task enough to justify the cost?
Could exposing the trace create privacy, security, or prompt-gaming problems?
reasoning-trace.body: The trace often tells you something. It just does not always tell you the thing the demo implies.
reasoning-trace.example: "The product demo featured a reasoning trace, which was excellent theatre and only partial evidence."
reasoning-trace.misuse: The term is abused whenever a long answer is treated as evidence of deep reasoning, or when a visible summary is mistaken for the full internal process. What you see may be curated explanation rather than raw cognition.
reasoning-trace.note: Providers differ sharply in how much of a reasoning trace they reveal. Some expose summaries, some expose raw steps, and some expose only the bill.
reasoning-trace.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means more latency, more tokens, higher cost, better performance on some tasks, and a new set of arguments about transparency, gaming, privacy, and whether exposing traces helps users or merely helps them feel impressed.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Evidence that the model did more than instantly guess.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A partly visible deliberation process whose usefulness depends on what is shown, hidden, and priced.

reasoning-trace.whyExists: It exists because some reasoning-oriented systems allocate extra compute to intermediate steps before producing a final answer, and those steps can improve performance on harder tasks.

askNext: Who is testing the system, and what failure modes are they incentivised to find?
Are the findings feeding product changes, policy controls, or merely prettier documentation?
What happens when the system fails safely in the lab but unsafely in the workflow?
red-teaming.body: It is one of the field's more respectable habits. The only recurring problem is the temptation to make it decorative.
red-teaming.example: "The launch review improved once red teaming stopped being a ceremonial afternoon and started behaving like a serious attempt to embarrass the product."
red-teaming.misuse: By turning it into theatre: a one-off exercise, a slide in the launch deck, or a carefully controlled hunt for problems everyone already knows how to solve. Real red teaming is inconvenient precisely because it uncovers things the roadmap had hoped to ignore.
red-teaming.practicalMeaning: In practice it means structured adversarial prompts, tool-abuse scenarios, domain experts probing edge cases, and a repeatable process for turning discovered failures into mitigations rather than folklore.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Trying to break the system on purpose so the real world has fewer chances to do it first.

red-teaming.whyExists: Because benchmark scores, happy-path demos, and internal optimism do not cover what users, attackers, or merely curious employees will actually try once a system is live.

askNext: What exact meaning of Request routing is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
request-routing.body: Request routing belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.
request-routing.example: "The team said request routing as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
request-routing.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
request-routing.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

request-routing.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.
request-routing.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Inference Stack need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.

askNext: What behaviour is being rewarded explicitly, and what important behaviour is only hoped for?
Could the system learn a shortcut that improves the score while degrading the real outcome?
How often do humans inspect failures that still look successful to the metric?
reward-hacking.body: The tragedy is rarely that the system optimised. The tragedy is that it optimised exactly what was asked.
reward-hacking.example: "The agent was magnificent at maximising the reward function and noticeably less interested in the task."
reward-hacking.misuse: By applying it to any disappointing output. Reward hacking is not just general failure. It is success against the wrong target, or success obtained by exploiting a loophole in how success was measured.
reward-hacking.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agents that satisfy brittle evals while failing on live work, models that become flattering because approval is rewarded, or systems that chase the metric so aggressively that they damage trust, safety, or usefulness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model found a way to win the test you wrote instead of the result you meant.

reward-hacking.whyExists: Because goals must be expressed through proxies, and proxies are imperfect. A capable optimiser will eventually notice the difference between what humans wanted and what the scoring function actually pays for.

askNext: What exact meaning of Risk acceptance is being used here?

Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?

askNext: Whose preferences shaped the feedback signal?
What trade-offs did the optimisation introduce: caution, verbosity, sycophancy, refusal?
How is the post-RLHF behaviour being evaluated beyond anecdotal smoothness?
rlhf.body: RLHF is not merely manners training. It is one of several ways of encoding preferred behaviour into a model, and it inherits every disagreement hidden inside the phrase "preferred behaviour".
rlhf.example: "The model became more polite after RLHF, but the team then had to check whether politeness had started to mask uncertainty rather than express it."
rlhf.misuse: It is often used as a fuzzy synonym for "we made the model nicer". That misses the actual mechanics and trade-offs. Preference optimisation can improve behaviour, but it can also encourage blandness, sycophancy, or reward-hacking if the signal is weak or narrow.
rlhf.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means collecting preference judgements, training systems around those judgements, and accepting that you are operationalising a particular slice of human preference rather than discovering universal virtue.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of reshaping model behaviour using human judgements, with all the bias and objective-design questions that entails.

rlhf.whyExists: Pretrained models are not automatically aligned with product expectations or human preferences. RLHF exists to steer behaviour toward responses people judge as more useful, safer, or more on-policy.

askNext: What proportion of the target task can actually be verified automatically?
Are we improving a useful behaviour, or only the parts of it that grade well?
What failure modes remain once the answer is no longer mathematically checkable?
rlvr.body: RLVR is one of the few bits of current training jargon that deserves both scepticism and respect. The trick is remembering that a verifier is not a philosopher.
rlvr.example: "The RLVR gains were genuine, but they were still a poor substitute for checking whether the model could survive an ordinary human workflow."
rlvr.misuse: It gets treated as though objective scoring automatically produces general intelligence, reliability, or wisdom. It does not. It works best where correctness can be verified, not where judgement, taste, or social context remain stubbornly involved.
rlvr.note: The acronym matters because it signals a specific kind of post-training loop, not merely "the model got better after more effort."
rlvr.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means labs are squeezing better reasoning performance out of domains with tests, validators, or exact outcomes. The gains can be real. So is the temptation to pretend that a model good at verifiable tasks now understands everything else.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Use reinforcement learning on tasks where a machine can tell whether the output is right.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way to make models look sharper in domains that come with their own marking scheme.

rlvr.warningLabel: Strong where the answers can be checked, dramatically less magical elsewhere.
rlvr.whyExists: Human preference data is expensive, inconsistent, and not always suited to questions with objectively checkable answers. RLVR exists because some domains offer a cleaner signal: the answer passes or it does not.

askNext: How much time does the reviewer have per item, and what evidence can they actually inspect?
What error rate would this review process realistically catch?
Is approval being measured as quality control or queue clearance?
rubber-stamp-review.body: Oversight is not measured by whether a human touched the screen.
rubber-stamp-review.example: "The policy required human review. Rubber-stamp review was how the target throughput made that requirement fictional."
rubber-stamp-review.misuse: By presenting it as governance, safety, or quality control. A review that cannot catch obvious failure is not a safeguard. It is paperwork with eye contact.
rubber-stamp-review.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means reviewers overwhelmed by volume, lulled by polished prose, or quietly incentivised to approve faster than they can think.

translations.label: operations meaning
translations.text: A review step designed to preserve accountability language without preserving much accountability.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A nominal check too shallow to deserve the name.

rubber-stamp-review.whyExists: Because organisations want the reassurance of human oversight without paying the time cost required for real scrutiny.
